subject: SF, data & Lima Labs, Inicial, biostimulants, Danisaraba & OAT
description:
* Discussion on SF, Thomas ultimately doesn’t believe in storing flowers, although recognising success
* On data and prediction, is sceptical on wether it is possible to forecast the price on the clock, but also what it means if Timaflor (with 15% of roses on Auction) decides to move their selling date; it would mean that they will get a better price in the future, and others can fill the gap for today, there is something there
* Not interested in sharing data, f.e. via drones and is against using his data. Very interested on wether drones could help with Pest & Diseases. If there is additional data from that, which can be used for forecasting, then we need to agree on that tightly
* Tried Inicial and sees that it works, perhaps slightly better. Definitely better for the process as checking would be easier because of smell chlorine, but if worth EUR 50k, he needs to convince himself on
* Is doing almost 100% IPM for spider mite, only uses 10L of Arima per year; lets offer Danisaraba
* Is pro biostimulants and has some that work really well, being croptimum that he puts in the water and agritecno for spraying. When bad weather, uses Cosmocel esp. for Downey
* Opportunity to try our Lida biostimulants and other OAT products

subject: Meeting Amit, Sham & Pradeep
description:
•	Investigated the process, met first with Krishna and then Pradeep who is spray manager
•	Pradeep is very happy, much more than with Dipnoy and Floralife, no customer complaints at all
•	Good protocol, diagram how product works to put on wall
•	Currently down from 150k for all production to 40k per day (two varieties)
•	Tendar (Bayer), Switch, completely stopped because of dipping! USP here
•	Sham from Subukia now in Flowers Isinya farm
•	Price from this week to EUR 0,009 + VAT per stem, try to push for more than only reds
•	Process: dipping twice (big tank or normal tilted tank), then on tables and pouring back leaked solution during the day; saving is 30% on efficiency, and that can be the solution being poured back
•	Blueish on Rhodos, happens when difference between hot and cold temperature, and then dipping
•	Trial the new poa tank; make some prototypes asap
•	Invoices to PJ Dave Flowers Limited, change previous and going forward

* They say still more expensive, but they like Chrysal and are ok to pay (look up price TOG75)
* Other opportunities are: colour dip for Craspedia, trials with Lianthus and Ascpleia
* TOG75 at 8ppm, do 1,5to 12ppm, then AVB 20ppm to 15 ppm, so 0,75ml/L
* Step by step, first GVB, then Optigyp, then AVB

subject: Visit Louisa & John
description:
• Mt. Kenya sea freight starting as well
• Blue Box - when ready inform them to get into it
• 230 greenhouses in Naivasha (practical side for Lima) - also weather influences can be very local (difference between two Naivasha farms in temp 2C and rain)
• Carnations no dip. For roses eventually no dip needed, but need STS. Chrysanthemums have leaf problems that need fixing - not sure what the results for sea freight are (John. Maybe Louisa would know). Same for alstroemeria from mt. Kenya (could also be interesting), 300-400k stems a week.
• Carnations (⅔) on Sea Freight. For Roses increase of volume now hold back by price of rose dip. Maersk shipper, CMA more expensive. Naivasha at 1,9 container a week YTD, 1 per month for Mt. Kenya (volume should go as well)
• Inicial - chrysanthemums, no indication → should follow up with TC’s and Louisa
• Bigot farm - taking step by step, from greenhouse towards pack house. Sea Freight is through Flamingo facility; maybe first container towards France?
• Data points for blue box. Speed of process out of country; number times flowers are touched; temperature movements (grading at 20c); VC will help get rid of condensation
• Vase life - CA rooms trial interest:
• Ethylene sensitivity (new varieties before planting). Breeders talk, but not good data or insights on that trials (too much ad-hoc now, should be more consistent for different weather conditions). Different treatments for different varieties as well
• Respiration rates for varieties

subject: Meeting Ashish & Andrew; commercial visit
description:
* Increasing on varieties, currently at 27 crops and all different varieties & colours
* Andrew Tubei moved from Sololo to Sand Pro

* Like the simplicity, no need for different water temperatures
* Find out dosage (Margriet), no problem to buy at 30kg’s; following Milima Ecuador that set the standard in colours, they need the standard five (light blue, light purple, light orange, light pink & light yellow - soft tones)
* Only they didn’t try yet the light purple, send sample; add the light green

subject: VISIT
description:
Sololo with Paul – Elizabeth. She wants to do storage of gyp in July for September (6 weeks) and in week 45 to 48 and week 52 to 2. These are the weak periods before the demand is high. We agreed the following (see my e-mail below)

Regarding your long storage for gyp we discussed the following treatments:

1.       Farm treatment (TOG 75 1,5 ml/l + TOG 3 + 0,25 ml/l Tvac - pulse; Optigyp + TOG 6 opening room) and then storage

2.       5 ml/l AVB + 0,25 ml/l Tvac pulse -> 30 gram Optigyp + GVB 1 ml/l opening solution then storage

3.       5 ml/l AVB + 0,25 ml/Tvac pulse -> storage then 30 gram Optigyp + GVB 1 ml/l opening solution

4.       5 ml/l AVB + 0,25 ml/l Tvac pulse -> 50 gram Optigyp + GVB 1 ml/l opening solution then storage

5.       5 ml/l AVB + 0,25 ml/l Tvac pulse -> storage then 50 gram Optigyp + GVB 1 ml/l opening solution in warm room

So the difference is in the pulse treatment products (TOG versus AVB) the moment of entering the flowers in the warm room and the dosage of Optigyp.

We feel that 50 gram per liter will help the flowers to keep the color and to open after storage in case they have been stored green.

You wanted from us our best guess what would be the best treatment as you want to do a ‘live’ trial in July. Then we would say either treatment 4 or 5.

I also would recommend to line the bunches with newspaper when storing, that will keep the humidity acceptable and will reduce the risk of mold or Botrytis.

Please let us know your thoughts than we can plan and help you with the execution.

As discussed we will do a mixing trial at Xpol in Holland to find the best recipes for the colors they want.

We also did mix Alstroemeria post harvest treatment for you (AVB 1ml/l – as you did not have this we used TOG 75 at 0,75 ml/l + 0.15 ml/l BVB + 1 ml/l RVB Clear). We look forward to hear the results from this trial.

For the dyes I will ask around a bit about the stability of the product, but please do not forget to stir the product before using with a clean stick.

The dyes do not seem to be very stable and differ per container. MvdP to check with Hans

Ole Engai with Paul we met with Steven and Mary Gyp and Delphinium farm with the best and neatest warming room I have every seen. They use the full Chrysal protocol and still have clean solutions at day 5. Very clean buckets and very well organized. No stem yellowing while their pH is at 3.9. This farm is also flocculating the AVB solution. We need to provide them with a STS indicator. Peter/Paul

For Delphinium we need to test AVB + GVB instead of AVB + RVB Clear as that will reduce the items they have to keep stock of. Peter/Paul

They would like to have a Post harvest training. Peter/Paul

subject: VISIT
description:
ACTION: RD ISSUES
Dear Margriet,

Good morning.
I spoke to the pack house Manager John and Assistant Steven .
I had a very good meeting again on Thursday with John of Xpol, GM Porini , Pack house manager and Assistant.
The meeting resolved that for Xpol the protocol must be followed and Porini were categorical that they don’t deviate which I doubt. John complained of serious mechanical damages and botrytis spots on their flowers. John will invite us at the airport to jointly assess the flowers at the airport.
The GM ad Team; are happy to dip 3 or 4 days old  flowers for Middle East. The results are good and customers are happy. They said they lost in  March/April a lot after dipping and then there was no market that’s why they prefer to dip only what they  have market.
The GM is very much in love with Rose dip and he even mentioned that Isinya don’t believe in it and they have lots of problems but he has clearly told them he is very happy with it. I understand Isinya have lots of problems with Rose dip and don’t like it [ We should investigate without quoting this source]
For now no need to escalate but we must stay vigilant with Porini!
We dipped some botrytis  infected and totally clean bunches of Ever red on Wednesday and jointly assessed them on Thursday. The clean flowers developed some spots of botrytis; 3 stems per bunch; there were 3 bunches. The flowers are in the vase life for observation at Porini and Ann will report back on the progress. To this end; the GM suggested if during such very wet periods whether Chrysal can maybe adjust the rate of Botreat for Porini [2800m ASL]???
The GM is more than happy to collaborate with Chrysal on more trials.
Hope to have sufficiently informed you
Kind regards
Paul

description: Met with the director -Jeroen and Nicholas the production manager. The farm is planning for expansion next year. They have not started using the Chrysal Inicial as they harvest dry, sleeve in the field and transport dry to the intake coldstore where packing happens. Then, the flowers are vacuum cooled and eventually to final cold store. The current biggest market is Middle East and see no need to hydrate the flowers. But they are dead sure that once they explore the European market there might be need for hydration. DFG had tried buying flowers from them, but Jeroen gave a high price to keep them off. They will be interested in sea freight/long storage trials any time soon. Jeroen is aware that we did sea freight trials with EAGA.

Bloemenvoeding sachets op basis van 20 Miljoen sachets per jaar:
355054431 Chrysal Essential sachet PP 3700*0.5L string INT.   0,0150 per sachet = € 15,00 per 1000.

subject: VISIT
description:
ACTION: RD ISSUES
Morning Margriet and Team,

Yesterday  we visited Porini with Ann to find out and investigate the recurring botrytis issues.

We found out complete protocol deviation.
1.	We found them dipping the day before flowers which had stayed overnight in the coldroom.
2.	We found Ever red which had been stored for in the coldroom in boxes for 3 days being dipped. 
3.	The packhouse management claimed that whatever old flowers they dip goes to the Middle East.
4.	The pack house management also claimed the XPOL orders are done with fresh flowers.
5.	They said they are aware of our protocol but they cannot pre dip as they have no orders.
6.	They said they have no claim at all from the Middle East even after dipping old flowers.
7.	For the wet flowers, they dip same day and want to ship the same day. Cold room is quite cold also the weather there is quite another so drying will not happen in hours.
8.	We were very clear they MUST dip CLEAN flowers but they dipping also flowers with spots and expecting them to go away but they are trying to say the scars are becoming active again.
9.	Had a call with Xpol ; they are visiting the farm today and they would like us to join again today and sort out this mess.
10.	As far as am concerned ; quality control is a big issue at the farm.
11.	There is a likely hood of old dipped flowers mixing with freshly dipped flowers and getting into Xpol orders.
What is the way forward here since we can clearly see there is a total protocol deviation without our approval?

subject: VISIT
description:
ACTION: DISCUSS CA PRODUCTS
Good afternoon Mike,

Great meeting you today and thanks for the time.

As we discussed, we see East African Growers as a key partner of Chrysal and wish to build on this partnership through increased dipping at your group farms.

We have been working closely with the R&D teams in Naarden and testing in Kenya different rose dip formulations in order to provide better "fit for purpose" recipes at different farms. For instance, in lower botrytis risk farms/weather conditions we can provide a different formulation and pass on that saving to you the grower, vs only using our premium rose dip during high risk periods.

Therefore as discussed, we propose to trial three formulations at Mahee next week - Rose Dip Premium, Economical and Basic. Paul will lead this test and I would appreciate the support of the farm to ensure the best success. After the results we can discuss the commercials to see what works best for you as a group.

subject: Mzurrie Group - Dipping Discussion
description:
Extensive discussion on efficiency with both Doreen & Andrew (now group MD) on the price vs efficiency issue.

Agreed the following pricing and efficiency targets:

- Target efficiency on our rose dipping of 100L per 10,000 stems (or more) on Standard Roses, and 150L per 10,000 stems on Spray Roses (increasing the number of stems continuously can lead to discussing the price)
- Based on these target efficiencies, we have agreed to reduce the price per stem to €0.009 + VAT per stem (standard roses) and €0.014 + VAT per stem (spray roses) accordingly
- If the dip is for sea freight or long storage, this will attract an additional €0.004 + VAT per stem to cover AVB, AVB Booster and Freshliner (and data logger in case of SF)

Sea freight shipping schedule shared and contact for Christo

subject: Visit
description:
ACTION: LS/AF DIPPING
Dear Seth

Good evening and many thanks for the business opportunity.

However as discussed there are issues to be highlighted.
1.	The farm plan is to store flowers in solution for long storage; am sure by close of business today the coldroom was full. It will be very difficult to store flowers tomorrow as there will be no space. We strongly  suggest to store the flowers in boxes and use Fresh liners like we have done before. This way you will be able to store a lot in the coldroom. You can still store them as raw material in the boxes and process them when you have orders.
2.	Flowers need to stay in STS overnight and not more. The plan currently is to keep them for  over 10 days and above in STS. We are using too much AVB solution per bucket; 4L is way too much; should be 2L. 
3.	Allocate one coldroom for storing the flowers with consistent temperature. At the moment the storage coldroom is being opened too many times. We need to avoid temperature fluctuations to avoid condensation. Ple

action: visit
Molo River (Mzurrie group) with Pauline, Paul and Matt

An unpleasant meeting that started with the accusation that we do not want to disclose the ingredients in the dip. They also do not want Jude at the farm anymore and Pauline could not give a good reason for that. Paul spoke with Pauline in private but then still she could not give a good reason. Now Peter II is there as their TA. We looked at their dipping process, there was no Rose Dipping taking place but with Dipnoy, An awful chemical smell was coming from that dip so probably it also had something against FCM in it. And the people around did not use any PPE. If they use Rose Dip they use that mainly for spray roses (70%) hence the poor efficiency. They use Rose Dip only for customers that require it like DFG, IPL and Xpol and for long storage.

Not sure what is going on there but it was not a friendly meeting. Tuesday we hopefully meet with Doreen at another Mzurrie farm and maybe we learn more about what is going on.

subject: antwoord gehad op mail van 5/3?
description:
Guten Tag, Herr Dekker,

ich bin Kundenbetreuer bei der Firma Chrysal und bin bei der Durchsicht unserer Kundendatenbank auch auf Ihr Unternehmen gestoßen. Ich habe auch versucht, Sie telefonisch zu erreichen, habe aber keine Antwort erhalten, daher diese E-Mail.

Wir haben bis 2022 miteinander Geschäfte gemacht, siehe Übersicht unten, und danach scheint die Zusammenarbeit leider beendet zu sein.

Ich würde mich gerne mit Ihnen in Verbindung setzen, um zu sehen, ob ich etwas für Sie tun kann und darf. Dies könnte telefonisch geschehen, aber ich wohne im Norden der Niederlande, so dass ich auch nach Aurich kommen könnte.

Ich sehe Ihrer Antwort mit Interesse entgegen.

Übersicht über die Aufträge der letzten Jahre:

subject: Visit
description:
Action: Chrysal Inicial Usage.
Met with Jeroen and Nicholas (production manager) to discuss the Chrysal Inicial dosing/mixing and usage. The farm has not started using the product as they are still putting up infrastructure for postharvest. Their ideal plan is to harvest dry, sort and bunch using a sorting machine and box the flowers, then transport to intake cold store via conveyer belt for precooling. The farm wants to use the minimum postharvest in the farm. 
They are really excited with the market as they are selling out every sellable stems. Their main market is in Middle East and Europe. Black Tulip Group had suggested to the farm to be sell them all the flowers but Jeroen declined.

Good afternoon Jay and Team,

Chrysal is looking for alternative ways of flocculating STS via a wetland.
We are doing these trials at Dummen Orange and we have engaged a consultant who is running this programme.
We are thus  requesting  if we could collect ALL USED AVB solution from your farm and pass it through the wetland chambers at Dummen.
If allowed , we will deliver a 1000L tank at your farm and keep collecting all the old/used AVB solution and then keep collecting and delivering to Dummen.
I looked forward to your positive response.

subject: Trialling RED CVs- leaf blackening
description:
ACTION:We did a red Roses trial, looking at leaf quality(blackening) 
Varieties ; Rhodos , Madam Red , Madam Cerise were used in the test.

We unpacked flowers after 5 days , dry in box in cold room.
This was after flowers staying in solutions for 5 days in cold room.

T1 - blackening leaves , very slight in Rrhodos 
T2 - blackening , more pronounced than T1 
T3- Slight Blackening leaves in all. More pronounced in Madam cerise 
T4 - more pronounced blackening in all 
T5- Very slight blackening 
T6- no blackening 
T7- blackening in Cerise , others okay 
T8- clean flowers, no blackening 
21st Jan we vase the flowers for further observations

subject: Operations Meeting
description:
Met with Natarajan as a courtesy visit to the group. He has been with the group for 16 years and oversees all operational aspects of the group (GM Flowers)

Following that met with Saran and team at the packhouse.

Key Stats and Takeaways:
-Advised he is happy with Inicial in the packhouse however we have a deficit of 9k vs PY that is unexplained.
- Long storage performance with Rose Dip has been good and there is an opportunity to grow further with the trials that were completed at Mahee. They are NOT happy with Floralife performance for long storage that will become a key focus as they move toward sea freight plans again. Overall we are up 32k LTM for rose dip services across the group
-Happy with valentines performance, and expect a strong year overall
-Large investments being made across the group with over $1.7m being invested in Maitri alone, and farm broke even after only 14 months (impressed)
-3800 employees within the flower division
- Expansion in Gyp across the industry has been something they are watching - Murara Plants he is happy with, but has told Danzigger they are not being forward thinking with respect to the hectarage expansion as it will impact the market
- Xcellence business we have an opportunity for them to give 100% to us. *Paul to follow up on this.
- Currently doing uptake dyes (quick - 20 mins) however they dont like that it is changing the stem colour. Have a bigger forecast for dyed flowers this year and would like to trial our available colours (including those not purchased by Xpol) for review.

subject: AH Emiel Bleekwilder komt langs
description:
Emiel is sustainable/kwaliteits man AH en komt langs. Rolf en Nora schuiven aan

23/7 goed gesprek gehad en 24/7 onderstaande gemaild aan emiel en patrick
Beste Emiel,

Nogmaals dank voor je komst naar Naarden en voor het leuke en open gesprek. Ik hoop dat we je een goed beeld hebben kunnen geven van Chrysal en hoe we nog meer zouden kunnen samenwerken. 

Hieronder een beknopte weergave van wat er is besproken en de actiepunten:

Tijdens het gesprek werd duidelijk dat gewasbescherming de hoogste prioriteit heeft voor AH. Aangezien het team aan het veranderen is, en op dit moment geen bloemenexpert bevat, zijn jullie zoekende naar beleid en visie. Duitse supermarkten gebruiken een “zwarte lijst” van gewasbeschermingsmiddelen, Chrysal zal een voorbeeld van zo’n lijst met AH delen.

Daar Chrysal veel kennis/experts in huis heeft kan zij zeker een ondersteunende rol spelen. Dit kan in Nederland maar ook in andere bloemen producerende landen zoals Kenia, Colombia, Turkije, Ecuador.  In een samenwerking is er altijd een vorm van ondersteuning maar extra support kan ook ingekocht worden. 

Mogelijke vormen van support:
• Advies over geschikte telers
• Audits bij telers en boeketmakers
• Winkel audits en vaasleven testen
• Trainingen voor toeleveranciers en winkelpersoneel
• Vaasleven testen
• Check koelketen met adviezen ter verbetering
Op dit moment maakt AH gebruik van de diensten van Flowerwatch voor het testen van de boeketmakers. Beeld is dat dit een eenduidige manier van testen is waar niet heel veel uit komt. Chrysal kan ondersteunen van teler tot consument en kan een bredere testaanpak inclusief aanbevelingen en follow up richting betreffende telers en boeketmakers bieden.

Ook de carbon footprint kwam aan bod. Chrysal kan bijdragen via:
• Overstap van poeder naar liquid voeding (45% lagere footprint), dit kan ook in een AH Private label – “AH Flowerfood by Chrysal
• Seafreight services, er staat een call met Gijs Heineke op 18 september gepland, wellicht dat jullie willen aansluiten?

Actiepunten:
• “Zwarte lijst” Duitse supermarkten delen(Chrysal). zie bijlage (Chrysal) ook een positieve lijst toegevoegd
• Voorbeeld verslag van een audit bij een teler en bij Bloemencentrale Hoorn delen (Chrysal) audit bij Bloemen Centrale zit in bijlage. Audit van teler in Kenia stuurt Rolf nog na.
• Duurzaamheidsbeleid delen (Chrysal) is reeds per separate mail verstuurd
• AH komt met lijst telers welke zij gebruiken/willen gebruiken voor hun assortiment.  Deze lijst kan dan samen met Chrysal worden doorgenomen. (AH & Chrysal)
• AH intern dit gesprek terugkoppelen en daarna vervolggesprek inplannen (AH & Chrysal)

Ik ben met vakantie t/m 25 augustus, maar Rolf is beschikbaar gedurende mijn afwezigheid. Ik hoor graag wanneer je het gesprek wilt voortzetten.

25/7 Emiel gesproken en intern al dingen aan bespreken en gaat tijdens mijn vakantie waarschijnlijk wel één en ander spelen. gegd om Rolf en/of Johan dan te benaderen. Rofl en Johan ook ingelicht en gezegd door te pakken nu "ijzer heet is".

subject: nog iets gehoord op mijn mail
description:
Hi Christian,

As discussed, this yesterday we think that the problems are caused by worn-out rolls. Especially the green soft foam rollers where not in the best shape. Bercomex has also replaced those rolls by improved rolls with better grip. This is article code 906-272382. We also shared a picture with you that on one of the binders there is a “spike” which is causing issues.

That you experience less issues with Vaselife  “not separating”  this could have to do with:
-	Their sachets are heavier, resulting in a bit better grip
-	We use PP recyclable plastics which seems to be a bit more slippery, resulting in less grip.
-	Maybe the use less pulling power to separate the sachets

We are so convinced that with the new roles the problem is solved that we offer you for one binder a set of new rolls to test. If this test is successful than I propose you also change the other rolls and we put our cooperation in the next phase.

Please let me know your thoughts.

subject: Task
description:
Set up a trial to compare the performance of the Gypmix and Floralife mix. The flowers are almost coming out of the warm room and the Gypmix has performed better than Floralife mix. 
The director had a feeling that the Gypmix was not giving enough weight, but the problem was growing as you could see thin stems in the field. Since, they have introduced fertigation and enough watering of Gypsophila in the field. 

When they carried out the trial on their own, they dosed AVB at 3mls/L and GVB at 0.5mls/L which was totally wrong (according to the farm staff they were advised by another guy). The farm stopped buying Optigyp and now are on another dextrose. Fingers crossed we retain them.

subject: Visit
description:
Physical audit on postharvest handling of flowers. 
Made a walk around the farm assess the areas that are causing damages-physical and pressure damages in the whole flower chain during and after harvesting. 
In the field the wrapping was quite tight in some varieties causing petals damages. The number of stems per wrap is varying. Some are double the number that is supposed to be put. We agreed to have a standardized number for all the varieties. 
In the packhouse, they should not stack flowers rather have the bunches put on the conveyor immediately after grading. 
In the cold stores, the arrangement is wrong as they putting the flowers pressed against each other. They were putting 16 buckets per rack Agreed that the flowers should be arranged spaciously opposed to the recommednded nine.

subject: Call
description:
Received a call yesterday in the evening from Caroline-director. The farm has been having issues with the Gypsophila weight which they thought was postharvest related. 
They called Floralife and tried Express 100 against GVB. She reported that there was significant weight gain in Express 100 treated flowers compared to GVB ones. Informed her that I will visit the farm this week on Thursday to find out more details because the weight gain in GVB has been impressive across different growers and it was an important aspect in the GVB adoption in the farms.

subject: Task
description:
Farm visit to open the long storage trial on Mums. 
Opened the Mums and T3 the best followed by T2 and T1 came in last. 
All the flowers/treatments were dipped in RoseDip without C7. They had complained of RoseDip with C7 scorching/bleaching the petals especially on the yellow variety. 
T1 was packed without sleeves, T2 was packed with sleeves and T3 was packed with sleeves and freshliners. Each treatment had flowers hydrated in plain water (current), TOG6 and TOG6+TOG L101. 
TOG+TOG L101 had the best leaves quality. 
The farm team was very excited and are looking forward to Mums storage when they have excess production. 

They had Octoflor visitung the farm the me day (buyer from Japan). The buyer spoke highly of RoseDip and would like his flowers to be dipped in it once he starts buying from the. The buyer is still new in the Kenyan market and will grow eventually. 

Doreen informed that Moo River Roses had bought an STS flocculation tank and requested for help in setting it up--to happen tomorrow. 
She also requested for RVBc trials on two varieties as they are having drooping challenges in Molo River Roses. The trial is set up for tomorrow. The farm is also in the processing of acquiring an RO machine. 
Agreed to carry out audits in the farms as soonest as possible. 
Bahati was using RVBc in the packhouse, probably with TOG6 supply issues? Will monitor it closely.

subject: Visit Katherine
description:
Action: plan for RVBc trial

* It was a pleasure meeting you this morning at Agriflora, thanks for your time.
* We discussed the quality complaints from Xpol with mixed bouquets and spray roses. The mono bunches spray roses do not have any issues but when mixed with other fillers often the spray roses have bent neck. Your current system is to hydrate all flowers overnight before processing. After processing these flowers are then immediately packed in boxes and placed in the export cold room. For mono bunches this is a very smooth system but maybe for the mixed bouquets this takes a bit too long. 
* We agreed that you would trial only hydrate the individual flowers for about 4 hours in your incoming cold room – then make the bouquets and then leave these bouquets in RVB Clear solution overnight and then pack. It is a big change for your logistical system but worth to trials as it might solve the complaints!
* We also saw that your guillotines are quite blunt, so good to sharpen them.
For Eucalyptus it is recommended to use either a Chlorine solution or re-used RVB Clear solution as that will clean the stems so that they will not contaminate the other flowers in the bouquet.

description:
Hi Nelmari,

Herewith the notes of the meeting of today and together with the overview/status of the various topics. 

•	Design -> The design has been approved by Bloomon and Chrysal has set up the article in the system and was in process of ordering paper. However during the meeting we found out we are not on the same page regarding the yearly volume. Ale agreed with Jorge a annual volume of 1.2M sachets per year while Nelmari was in the understanding that current volume will be split between Chrysal and Floralife. No use to put negative energy in a “who is right story” let’s put energy in how to solve this.
Actionpoints:
-	Come back with the yearly volume(Nelmari)
-	After receiving the yearly volume re-calculate the price (Ale)  question: Nelmari you said we where more expensive than Floralife. But I just checked according to me we are cheaper than Floralife. Floralife price is € 0,064 while we offered € 0,062. Can it be this price was also not known by you?
-	Costs of design. I checked the costs in meantime and the design consists out of 4 colors, where the White color is not counted as a color. Total cost with these 3 colors including al the re-designs is approx. € 3000, to be paid by Bloomon.
•	Aquasub dosing units -> Bloomon has 2 test dosing units of Chrysal on loan. The original plan was that those units would be used as a reference to develop new dosing units by Bloomon/Ruigrok. The plan was that the test units could be used till December 2024. In meeting of 19 december Jorge asked if test dosing units also could be used in January 2025. I proposed than to get them back after the peak of Valentines day.  In meantime the loan units dosing units have been adapted and Nelmari would check with Matthew what his view is on the progress.
The costs of the units are € 1890,00 excl. VAT per unit. Also delivered 2 weeks ago a new Dosing pump to Ruigrok which will be add-ed the cost of this is € 347,00.

The account budget is   € 1,772.43 and can be used for purchasing these or new dosing units.

subject: AUDIT
description:
ACTION: AUDIT 
Did a thorough audit.
Hi all,

Attached are some preliminary results from what we found out today. As usual, the more you know the more questions you ask!

Sabina is going to set up some further trials tomorrow, so that we have some more answers before Margriet is here next week to discuss them.
We would like to know 2 things: 
•	What is causing wilting and bleaching the stems on vase in Holland as seen in Robert’s photos. Is it something other than bacteria? Paul has done the swab checks so we will know if we have a hygiene problem soon. Is the low EC / low pH causing visible bleaching, water uptake or effecting vase life? 
•	Is the EC of the rose dip scorching the flower heads.

Test 1.
To rule out variability on the starting solution of RO+RVB we are going to follow a single batch the whole way through the process. Today’s results used solution from 2 different days.
If we find out that the starting solution is very different to what we checked today, we will need to consider using a dosing machine to stabilize the basic packhouse post-harvest solution. We will discover if this is a material problem or not. What we do know is that the low EC of RO water makes it very pH volatile. Is it a real consideration to switch the packhouse post-harvest and dipping solution source over to flocculated lake water?

Test 2.
We will make 2 solutions – one with RVB and one with ‘RVB soft’ which is designed for RO water.
We will test 6 varieties from the same harvest batch in each solution: Moonwalk, Pink Athena, Athena, Red Calypso, Mysterious, Mandarin. All selected by observing which have more bleaching and scorching issues.
We will split each set, some flowers through dipping and some without.
We will recheck the solutions after 24hours in the cold store.
This will give us a 4- way result on pH, EC against a known starting point.

 

Test 3.

We will also check the pH and EC of the Rose dip to confirm today’s figures.
 

Test 4.
We will put the flowers through vase life, to visually look for the scorching and the bleaching, and wilting.
 
Thanks to Paul for today’s assistance and I look forward to finding out more.

subject: Visit
description:
Farm visit. Met with the director, operations in charge and the production manager. 2 weeks ago, the farm experienced some strong winds and 2.5ha of Roses had the whole greenhouses brought down by the winds. No Roses are being harvested now as the whole structures were brought down leaving the crop exposed. 
The Gypsophila fields not looking as impressive as they used to be. You can't tell what the issue is with the farm despite the wind damages. 
To keep monitoring to know how the farm fairs on.

subject: Visit
description:
Farm visit together with John. Met with the production manager. Took a crop walk around the farm. Their current production is not enough for the demand they have. They are forced to short the customers. 
The field is having some issues in growing. Some patches in the field are getting discoloured and stunted/uneven growth. Agreed that Patrick should visit the farm and discuss the Bio-stimulants to help boost the crop. They are almost completing a 3.5ha extension of Mums planting. The total will be 7ha. They still on dry harvesting, grading and packing in the field and transporting the boxes into the intake cold store for vacuum cooling.
They have not started using postharvest. Probably after IFTEX with new customers' demands. 
There is a plan to plan 10 acres of Hypericums.
They are also thinking about Gypsophila though not any time soon.

Key Stats and Takeaways:
-Advised he is happy with Inicial in the packhouse however we have a deficit of 9k vs PY that is unexplained.
- Long storage performance with Rose Dip has been good and there is an opportunity to grow further with the trials that were completed at Mahee. subject: CALL
description:
ACTION: WETLAND PROGRESS
Hi both, just to keep you updated on progress. Have just spoken with Paul on this as well.

So last week the first samples were taken well, waiting on results now.

Then on Thursday, Labour Day holiday here in Kenya, there was flooding at Dummen-Orange and the wetland was a mess.

Long story short, after a lot of work (I'm there every day and feel like and employee, minus the salary!) we've got the system sorted and now understand where all the water is coming from the various farm operations. It seems before this no-one really understood. Now myself, Roddy (new manager) and Sam (maintenance) jointly understand everything. 

I'm going to let the wetland settle down for a week to let the mess they made pass through the system. This will allow us to also see initial results from the Deactivation Pit samples we took. And allows me to go and return to the UK (Sat to Weds).

Then we'll run the tests properly for a full week.

Which, Paul tells me, will coincide with your next visit Margriet? So despite the setback there are silver linings (pun intended!

subject: Meeting with Doreen
description:
Action: Peter check-in farm manager when back leave. Share Xpol dye recipes

* As always it was a pleasure to meet you, thank you for your time.
* We discussed how you could further improve your rose dip efficiencies at the farm. An idea might be to harvest specially for the Rose Dip orders for the customers. Many farms do that already and that reduces the waste very much and also improves the speed of processing, combined with a better dip efficiency! So, let’s try that and see how we can make you a better offer. These are your efficiency numbers for the past 5 months.
* As agreed, you will share your potential Chrysal RVB Clear consumption with us so that we can give you a good offer for that.
* Peter will check with the production manager when he is back from his leave about the AVB flocculation process at the farm.
* Once we have the Xpol dye recipes we will share them with you.
* They currently dip for Xpol, Mestergronn and Agrotropic. Still export a lot to Russia but do not dip because of the price. They also do a lot of storage up to 4 weeks for the peaks, which goes very well, then they use Rose Dip and freshliner. They re-use the freshliner and wash them with sporekill in between.

Farm visit with Raphael (TA) TO Tambuzi Turaco farm to start bio stimulant trial. The farm is in Mt Kenya Alstroemeria. they have uprooted some Alstroemeria area now at 2.4Ha and planted spray roses now at 3.63 Ha. subject: Farm visit
description:
Action: Start the trial
Farm visit with Raphael to start Chrysal Element and Alpha trials.  In this trial, we have 3 treatments: Control, Alpha and Alpha + Element. We did Phytotoxicity test at 1.5ml/L of each product. We also tagged 25 stems on each treatment. We will observe our parameters of Shoot numbers and Length on these tagged stems.  The trial will take approximately 60 days to completion. We will also do Vase life test on the tagged stems.

subject: Visit
description:
Farm visit. Met with Patrick (farm manager). He explained that PCPB are now requiring the oil-based plant extracts to be registered whether for use in postharvest or preharvest. PCPB claim that companies are importing such and using the products for postharvest control of whiteflies. Thus, everything has to be registered even if oil based. 
They also requested for postharvest training as it is becoming one of the requirements by external auditors. Th trainer has to be registered under NITA. The training will involve Leafshine and Seal. 
He asked if Chrysal Africa will have an open day any time soon.

subject: Gedr Langeveld bv
description:
Bezoek Gebr. Langeveld – Sassenheim
Vandaag een goed gesprek gehad met Gebr. Langeveld in Sassenheim. Oorspronkelijk een familiebedrijf van drie broers. Twee van hen zijn in Nederland in de zaak gestapt, terwijl de derde ruim 30 jaar geleden naar Amerika is verhuisd om daar de Amerikaanse tak op te zetten. Inmiddels is het bedrijf flink gegroeid en werken er zo’n 100 mensen, waarvan 10 in Nederland en de rest verdeeld over drie vestigingen in de VS.

Het bedrijf is inmiddels deels overgenomen door een investeringsmaatschappij. Ze verschepen containers vol bloembollen en -knollen naar Amerika, en daarnaast ook hardwareproducten zoals potten voor grote tuincentra en grote bouwmarkten verkopen .klanten bestand van 8.000 klanten.  Ze hebben ook nog een apart bedrijf in waterplanten voor vijvers en dergelijke.

Volgens hen gebruiken de kwekers en boeketmakers daar weinig tot geen voeding. Dat biedt mogelijk grote kansen voor Chrysal – zeker op het gebied van bloembolvoeding.

De inkoper reist over drie weken naar het hoofdkantoor in de VS. Ik heb aangeboden om samples van onze voeding langs te brengen – die neemt hij dan graag mee. Denk aan voeding voor tulpen, narcissen en dahlia’s. Afspraak gehad na offerte uitleg over producten.

Dear Margriet and Team,
 
I just had a good meeting with Tom again this morning.
He is very keen to start dipping and doing long storage.
He feels that the Rose dip service is hectic ad complex but have made him understand it so now very clear to him.

Kind Regards,

Eigenaar Daily Flowers,
Daily Flowers plaatst op drukbezochte locaties in Nederland zogeheten Automated Flower Stores – innovatieve, onbemande bloemenautomaten met ruimte voor 24 premium boeketten, elk voorzien van een zakje Chrysal snijbloemenvoeding. subject: VISIT
description:
ACTION: VISITAND FOLLOW UPS
Courtesy visit. They are struggling less with their Asters, they have found specific post harvest treatments per variety:

· Teeny tiny Pink – RVB Clear

· Teeny tiny Blue – RVB Clear, but it does not open so they harvest more mature but then have more Botrytis

· Double Date Pink – RVB Clear + Florissant 210 (half dose) + TOG 75

· Pink Flash – RVB Clear + Florissant 210 (half dose) + TOG 75. They still have some leaf yellowing but now only at day 6 in the vase life. They found that their manganese levels were too high. They have adjusted their fertilizer.

Phlox : RVB Clear + 210 + AVB

Clematis: RVB Clear

Solidago: RVB Clear (works better than Inicial, they see a difference in the leaf quality)

subject: Visit
description:
Visit with Margriet. The directors complained about GVB supply which delayed. They agreed to share a forecast of the expected usage of their postharvest products for the next months (3-6). 
They are being audited for FSI certification by September. They are Global GAP certified. They got the best report for the Xpol special dyes order for Norwegian national day. They requested if they can be given the exclusivity to supply that line yearly. For normal dyes, they said the flowers shrink. For the different STSs, AVB Pro was equally good. Agreed to repeat the STSs trials with normal and pulsing treatments.

subject: Visit
description:
Visit with Margriet. Met with Sarah. The farm had quality issues with roses sent via cape. They were following the sea freight protocol in full. Some varieties that were doing good via Red sea are performing dismally via Cape route. 
Highlighted some of the issues that might cause the quality issues which included; varietal performance, postharvest hygiene, time taken to unload the flowers after the container arrives in Europe. 
Discussed if dipping in cold RoseDip could cause any petals quality issues. We planned for dipping in coldstore and ambient temperature and see if there is going to be any differences in the petals. 
During the trial will carry out a postharvest audit. The flowers will be shipped by Maersk via Cape route.

subject: Farm visit
description:
Action: Follow up on trial
Farm visit with Amos (TA) to start the bio stimulant trial. together with the farm team we did start two trials, in Gypsophila and Lisianthus. In Gypsophila, the main issue is mortality caused by soil pathogen and in Lisianthus is calcium deficiency causing leaf tip burn. in Gypsophila trial, we are testing Chrysal fundament and Avenue, with 3 set of treatments to help the plant with strong and mass roots and also uptake and check the effect on mortality. in Lisianthus, we're testing Chrysal Forte, Avenue and Fundament in 3 set of treatments to help in rooting and nutrients or uptake. on this day we did first application of Chrysal Fundament on both trials. the trial will continue for 16 weeks.

subject: Farm visit
description:
Action: Give samples to the farm.
 Farm visit with Antonio and Josien. meeting with Jeroen, the farm owner. Mainly growing different varieties of Chrysanthemums with 3.5 Ha under production and another 3.5 Ha being planted. The farm has a potential of 80 Ha of expansion. Main problem is soil with high Sodium and poor aeration leading to water logging. as result some areas has stunted growth. Also, magnesium toxicity is a problem. Because of the distance the farm is located and other logistics, we agreed to give the farm samples of our stress product to trial. We will also give samples of Fundament and Avenue for trial to help the crop establish strong roots.  We will provide all the information like dosage and application frequency then follow up with the farm to check on results.

subject: Farm visit
description:
Action: Plan for trials.
Farm visit with Antonio and Josien. meeting Daniel Karuga, the technical manager. The farm is 28.5 Ha under rose production, mainly standard roses but also small section with spray roses. 1.5 Ha of Chrysanthemums mainly from interpant, Dummen orange and Lathy flora breeders and 0.1Ha of Alstroemeria.  The farm has planted some gypsophila. the farm is currently using Full root, Bio vegetal and Tecamin max bio stimulants. Because of the high altitude, Botrytis is a major challenge with weekly sprays of fungicides. we agreed to trial Chrysal Fortify in additional to the weekly fungicides spray. In this trial, we will do humidity chambers and Vase life trials. The farm wants to increase stem diameter in their alstroemeria. we will trial our Alpha Fundament and Avenue.
On Powdery mildew, mainly using Sulphur, Meltatox(Dodemorph Acetate) () Impulse (Spiroxamine), Nimrod (Bupirimate) and Polar (Polyoxin). agreed to conduct Gatten pre commercial trial after the Botrytis season is over.

wat is beste behandeling.  1/8 komt de kweker naar NL toe

16/7 onderstaande voorstel gemaild:
Hi Bob,

Naar aanleiding van ons telefoongesprek gisteren, waarin je vertelde dat jullie Delphinium en Hortensia’s van Flora Delight Kenia gingen importeren, heb ik één en ander uitgezocht.

Hortensia’s

Onder andere kwekers uit Zuid Amerika hebben goede ervaringen met het gebruik van onze Freshliner en willen niet meer zonder. De Freshliner is speciaal ontwikkeld om de kwaliteit te behouden tijdens transport en biedt daarnaast talloze voordelen. In het bijgevoegde productsheet staan deze beschreven.

Deze hebben we in 3 verschillende uitvoeringen.  De prijzen waar ik ze voor kan leveren staan erachter.  Een aantal samples kan ik jullie uiteraard sturen.

742521090	Chrysal FreshLiner bag 250pcs 500mm INT	95,25
742523090	Chrysal Freshliner bag 150pcs 1100mm NL	145,07
742520090	Chrysal FreshLiner bag 250pcs 750mm INT	142,04

Daarnaast raden we aan om de Hortensia’s te behandelen met Chrysal Professional 3 of Grow 40.  Grow 40 heb je sowieso nodig als je ze ook wil aanvoeren op de veiling. Zie product sheets voor de omschrijving van deze artikelen.

We agreed with the farm to calibrate the meter and recheck.

subject: Farm  visit
description:
Action: Plan for trials.
Farm visit with Antonio and Josien. Meeting with Sunil, the farm manager. the main farm is 40Ha and has started a new site which 1km away from the main farm, with 4.5ha already planted with roses. an additional 4.5ha to be planted in July 2025. Mainly doing 40% direct market in UK and Middle East and 60% through Auction. They have a total of 20 standard varieties. Mian challenges include thrips mostly in February, March, April, October and November. Botrytis is also a problem especially during rainy season. Currently they are doing replanting in 6.5Ha in main farm which will continue until June. Because of replanting in main farm and the new planting in the new site, the farm would want the crop to start with strong roots and as such we will trial our Chrysal Fundament an Avenue to help in root development and uptake of nutrients. we will also set a Chrysal Fortify trial to help in strengthening the plants against Botrytis. the farm is currently using Hicure twice per crop cycle to help in sprouting, growth and during extreme hot or cold weather. we will set up Chrysal Alpha trial to compare with Hicure.

subject: Farm Visit
description:
Action: Follow up for trials
Farm visit with Josien and Antonio to Kariki Naivasha. Meeting with Gloria, the production manager. Kariki Naivasha is part of the Marginpar  group. The farm 46 Ha and mainly growing summer flowers, Hypericum 14Ha, Eryngium 15Ha, Agapanthus 3Ha, Gypsophila 4 Ha and Delphinium 8Ha.  Soil borne pathogen is one of the main problems across all the crops crop -Fusarium and phytophthora leading to high crop mortality. they do monthly drench of Trichoderma and Promocarb &Fosetyl Aluminium. in Hypericum the main issues are Rust and Nematodes, normally drench Fluopyrum for the nematodes. Thrips, Caterpillar and Fusarium are main issues with Eryngium while Mites and Fusarium in Delphinium. Agapanthus has issues with botrytis post-harvest. Again, high Sodium bicarbonate of 230ppm (ideally should be 120-220ppm) and Hardpan soil is major problem. the farm mainly uses borehole water. the farm also experience dormancy in Eryngium which makes the plants goes up to 34 weeks from which ideally should be around i6 weeks. As a solution, we will try Chrysal Fortify applied in field in Agapanthus to make the plants strong and observe post-harvest effect on Botrytis. For the soil problem we will trial Fundament and Avenue to make the plants have strong roots. Gloria will confirm when we can start the trial once he has discussed with Bobby, group agronomist who was on leave at the time of the visit.

subject: Farm visit
description:
Action: Follow up on the trials.
Farm visit with Antonio and Josien. meeting with Tom, operational director and Eric, technical manager. the farm has two location which is 5 km apart, South farm(13Ha) and North farm(12Ha). Mainly growing Hypericum (accounting for 80%) Kangaroo, Lysmachia and Eryngium. The main market is UK, Japan, Australia and UAE (50%), Auction (40%) and Direct clients in Netherlands (10%). Main problem Root disease (Fusarium, and Rhizoctonia and length (Eryngium), Aphids and Thrips (Lysmachia) and post-harvest Botrytis (White Hypericum)-the do weekly spray of fungicide for hypericum Botrytis. Currently there is ongoing Chrysal Alpha, Fundament, Avenue and Element trial in Eryngium to help in Length and production. In week 22, we will start Fortify trial for the Botrytis in Whit Hypericum applied in field and observed post-harvest. Later in week 24 we will again start Chrysal Fundament, Avenue and Alpha in Lysmachia to help the plant in uniformity and to promote generative growth- Lysmachia crops stays in vegetative growth for long without turning into generative growth.

subject: Lauren visit
description:
Complaint was on order to superflora 
the farm Receive flowers , dip raw , precool over night 
Then process
A week ago , DFG requested to change to DFG Process 
Grade , process and dip , place in cold room overnight , put clear sleeve  and pack, while inside coldroom 
This has not changed...
Flowers are dipped and left to dry overnight on the grading floor , urguably to dry.. they were still wet...  
This,  all a deviation to protocol.
Farm admits their process from the field also cause damages.
Wrapping nets do cause damages too.
Farm to look into this. 
Dipping container too small  for  raw product. We have a bigger 70L container but only suitable for finished product. (TA worried on efficiency) 
DFG says we charge 20K stems minimum so solution efficiency should not arise?....
We saw lots of thrip damage on flower tips,  ect to mechanical damages, even in this cold moment.
Flowers are quality checked again after grading before placing in cold room, resulting to too much handling.
For superflora , flowers will henceforth be bunched, sleeved , dipped and placed in cold room. We saw very poor flower habndling by bunchers and deforliators too,commited themselves to improve

subject: Visit
description:
Met with Nicholas. Discussed RoseDip as it is now wet. The farm is currently using Botrytis Bloc Bundle against Botrytis. The estimated cost per stem according to him is Kshs 1.72. The farm is planning to expand 7ha of new varieties for spray roses. The production is expected to come in September/October. The farm will be 100ha after the expansion. For the first flash, they need to be treated as they are sensitive to Botrytis. 
For RVBc, it was put on hold as they are currently on TOG 10 AND TOG 75 on spray roses.  It will be considered as an alternative to TOG 10 should need be. 
On Gypsophila, we discussed the new dyes; both uptake and dip. They are interested in uptake dyes. For dip dyes, they feel it takes time to dry the flowers especially if it is an urgent order and the way of disposing the balance. 
They mix TOG 3 with uptake dyes and reuse for 4 days. Recommended GVB. No dextrose is added in this solution. 
The farm has uprooted Alstoemerias because of ROI.

Action:  Plan for Chrysal Forte bio stimulant trial.
Farm visit with Antonio and Josien. meeting with Ivan Freeman, the director. The farm is 20 Ha mainly supplying their flowers to Middle East, West Africa, Eastern Europe and Australia. the crop Cycle is between 70-90 days depending on the varieties. We did a small presentation on Chrysal pro actives i.e.  Fortify, Forte, Alpha, Avenue and Fundament. Ivan was interested in Chrysal Forte for the Calcium and Fortify to help in activating plant defence mechanism against botrytis. The Fortify trial we agreed to set up in October while the Forte trial to done soon.
The farm is doing Dipnoy dip as anti-Botrytis dip and are happy with the results.

subject: SF OMNI CONTAINER
description:
Hi Matt and Paul,
As discussed, please find attached photos of the quality issues on the first sea freight shipment to Omni flora(52 days transit from farm to offloading of container). During today’s call, the team in Omni flora mentioned they would compile a detailed report next week and share it with us. Their initial comments and concerns were as follows:
1.	Damages and drying/browning tips on the bright colors (Athena & Pink athena).
2.	Discoloration on Patz and Smiley, with Patz specifically showing a faded color.
3.	Blackening and bluing observed on the Madam Reds.
They estimate a 40-50% rejection rate, but will provide a full report later.
Additionally, please note that they have confirmed two containers will be arriving simultaneously at their site tomorrow.
I’ll keep you updated as we receive more information, but that’s all for now. Looking forward to hearing your thoughts.
Kind regards,

Sarah.

subject: Meeting Santosh & Elias
description:
* Still have botrytis and rejects so need for TC to evaluate
* See if the tracer is there and check for that; the botrytis when visibible is big so looks like not dipped

* One harvest, 7:30am to 12:30pm
* Harvest everything that is ready, only sometimes go into the afternoon
* Sometimes break the flower if not needed, and cut when planning for the flush
* End of the day, we know the production
* Orders:
* 	(Direct) Standing orders 20/25% (year-round), deliver once, twice or thrice a week depending on customer
* 	(Direct) Weekly orders are more seasonal, spray and intermediate is 100%
* 	(Indirect clock) only Rhodos goes 30% to clock
* When production is lower, because of communication they are able to discuss and have provision to fulfil order
* When excess production, then communicate availability to customers every morning; team sits every morning to do this; 80% will mostly be sold through this
* Tomorrows orders fulfilled yesterday production
* Clock is not last resort, because if the demand is not there, no need to send to clock for low prices
* Prices:
* 	Year-round prices, based on experience, negotiate on volume, anything constantly. Standing orders not different from ad hoc
* Basically two prices, standard price and then peak prices for excess and clock volume, not for standing orders
* Only thing you can play with, is 30% Rhodos and then any higher production
* Orders receive on emails: but for all customers there are whatsapp for follow up. Email goes to the system and orders sent to packhouse; price is already there so its up for the packhouse to confirm; all these discussed daily to see how to fulfil including speaking to customer if possible to deliver next day
* Weekly and standing is priority 
* 100% forecast accuracy would mainly save time and effort, be more efficient on production teams, etc. also confidence from clients, and potentially better rewards for that. Reputation is very important, no extra price but happy customers
* Customers
* 	60-70 customers coming and going
* 	Better to have 40 to 45
* 	25 are standing orders customer
* 	Customers are varietal, not so much reputation to take more
* Reputation is key, that is most important
* Around 70% accurate, to 80%; but the drastic changes can really throw; drastic is the problem but then increase to 85% great
* Supervisor all together to senior supervisor, the final forecast from senior supervisors (6); weekly on Thursday; but also a four-week and then for seasons the flushing planning. During the week just production actuals
* Forecast is the most difficult, you have events and then suddenly scorching f.e.; blind shoots (less light), no sprouts (nobody knows); blind shoots 25% more or less; so forecasting clouds (radiation) & min. temps
* Norway weather and google weather
* Weather for next 7 days, then improve on spray regime and speak to customers
* Spray daily basis change, just which product to use for that greenhouse, so either use AI or not
* Better scouting on pest & disease could work very well
* Spray program for history and proposal, the weather is this so do this
* Gatten not too interested, rhodos resistant powdery. Main is downey botrytis, thrips & mites (fcm & caterpillar)
* def interested spray tool

subject: Offerte maken
description:
Offerte maken nav bezoek 9 juli

10/7 volgende gemaild
Beste Hans,

Leuk je gisteren persoonlijk ontmoet en gesproken te hebben. 

Zoals gisteren afgesproken hierbij de aanbieding die ik je kan doen.


Bloemenvoeding zakjes:
 
Chrysal Essentials. Deze formule is speciaal ontwikkeld naar aanleiding van de vraag om betaalbare kwaliteit te bieden voor de veranderende boeketsamenstelling in de Discount Retail sector. Kwaliteit van deze formule zit tussen de Chrysal Supreme Biobased formule en de Fleur Vital formule in.  Deze voeding voldoet uiteraard ook aan de wettelijke regelgeving mbt ingrediënten en verpakkingen en voldoet ook aan de richtlijnen van de BVL en kan dus ook in Duitsland gebruikt worden.  Wordt ook in mono PP geleverd. Zie ook bijlage
 
Deze formule is vanaf heden verkrijgbaar in:
- poeder Universeel
- 0,5 ltr
- los en String

Normaal is de MOQ 5Miljoen zakjes per jaar maar wil voor jou een uitzondering maken. Met in achtneming van je jaarafname in 2024 als referentie kan ik je aanbieden: 
 
Artikel 355054431 Chrysal Essential sachet PP 3700*0.5L string INT. € 60,31 = € 0,0163 per zakje

Een liter zakje hebben we (nog) niet in deze formule maar ik kan je wel de Chrysal Universeel of Chrysal Rose aanbieden. 
343064431 Chrysal Universal PP sachet 2100*1L string INT € 63,21 = € 0,0301 per zakje
348064431 Chrysal Rose PP sachet 2100*1L string INT     € 65,61 = € 0,0312 per zakje

 
Transport en Retail fase:
 
Bloemen en planten moeten tijdens transport en in de winkel fris en gehydrateerd blijven. De Professional producten van Chrysal verbeteren niet alleen de kwaliteit maar helpen ook om uitval te verminderen en de houdbaarheid te verlengen.

Hier kan ik je in de T-bag versie volgende aanbieden:
 
 392791110 Chrysal Prof. 2 Premium T-Bag 1850*2L EN 87,69 = € 0,0474 per T-bag.

Bovenstaande prijzen staan vast tot 31 juli 2026 en gelden alleen als we jullie preffered supplier worden. 
 
Ik hoor graag je reactie

subject: aantallen PL, nog iets gehoord
description:
Hi Frank,

Denk stoor je even niet aan de telefoon want je zal wel druk zijn met Int. Women’s day.

Ons product management team wees mij op het volgende: voor een Privat label is de minimale jaarafname voor een 1Ltr zakje 500.000 stuks per jaar. Dit betekent dus als wij een Privat label opzetten dan wordt er voor minimaal 500.000 zakjes film besteld en dan moet deze binnen het jaar worden afgenomen.

Nu heb ik gekeken naar jullie aantallen voor de Universeel, Rose en Tulp variant en dan doen jullie per variant er ongeveer 2,5 tot 3 jaar over om tot een afname van 500.000 stuks per variant te komen.

Kun je mij, na Vrouwendag, de te verwachten aantallen per variant doorgeven voor 2025 en 2026 dan kan hier eens naar kijken.

Hoor het graag van je.

14/3 reminder gestuurd

24/3 gebeld en gesproken. Hij kan geen aantallen geven, als aantallen niet goed genoeg zijn dan maar op zoek naar andere leverancier. Gezegd da ik wel beetje kan rommelen maar 2,5 tot 3 jaar is beetje teveel van goede  Zo afgesproken dat als design klaar is we het over aantallen gaan hebben. Opslag van foliw wil hij evt. wel voor betalen.

subject: VISIT
description:
ACTION: VIIST
•	Expanding Roses from 2ha to 5.5ha
•	Gypso will remain 13.4ha.
•	This hot period is difficult with GVB, but they are managing.
•	They keep adding some biocides to stabilize GVB, but they avoid keeping the flowers for too long.
•	GVB is still the preferred post-harvest product due to price but they would switch to TOG 3 if price was good.
•	German said TOG 3 opens the flowers much faster than GVB and has  better weight.
•	GVB is very inconsistent.
•	Colour of GVB matters- clear is the best!
•	Chrysal should supply few batches as too many batches means too many trials once received. If we are to supply more than 3 batches we must alert him beforehand.
•	Chrysal must hasten the order process to avoid delays.
•	Discussed doing pulsing for gypso but he prefers cocktails as he doesn’t want mistakes by his Team.
•	Afri Flowers will have a big stand at IFTF and the sales team from Ecuador will be present.
•	Discussed reduced prices offered to them by Matt and he will look keenly at them and get back to us. He always keeps some AVB just in case TOG 75 runs out.
•	Still happy with Inicial and RVBc and volume will grow from next year with the new expansion.
•	Gave a sample of GVB SW for trial. He will get back to us with the results.

WAGENIGEN PPT 
Water uptake of cut flowers (Flower water balance) 
Wilt- higher water loss than uptaken(negative water balqnce).
Can also be due to broken cells of the leaf due to age, leaves fail to hold water anymore
Plants also recover from water loss ...
Cutflower has passive water uptake 
A plant has roots , there fore passive and active water uptake.
Plants ussually recover during the night when only active water uptake happens 
Water uptake for cutflowers decrease with days due to vascular blocking with time in the vase.
Stomata in the leaves close due to less transpiration hence slowly end of vaselife 
Water balance in the vase determines when the flower wilts.
Xylem vessels transport the water up the flower
Water moves from one vesel element to the next through the pits that contain a membrane with a gel like substance. This makes uptake very slow and possible blockages by 1-bacteria and their excreted products.
Dead bacteria also blocks the vascular system.
Nit all flowers are sensitive to this blockage.(e.g monocots)
Bacteria numbers are really what count , not species.
Sugars that leak into the water from cutflower promote to the quick multiplication of vacteria 
Biocides and low pH supresses bacteria growth 
Some crops are sensitive to bacteria while others not (eg carnation)
Roses, syringa and astilbe are very sensitive to bacteria blockage  
2- air embolisms
Air doesnt go from element to element and it repairs itself by air dissolving into the water 
Cold water facilitates air dissolving into the water.
High positive pressure also helps to dissolve the air into the water.
Blockage at the cut surface (eg by bacteria) may create under pressure in the xylem vessels increasing air hence embolism.
Cacitations (is it water or air) can be measured on the stem using a microphone... 
Remedies 
Use cold water to help water dissolve more quickly 
Suffactants (gets rid of air that maybe in one of the pit membrane) , citric acid also help break the air
Citric is thought to loosen the pit membrane and facilitate water movement.
PHYSIOLOGICAL BLOCKAGES 
This is due to enzymic activity in the plant.
Some remedies include using Hot water to deactivate the enzymes. This can be risky you have to be careful on temperature , not to 'cook' the flower. 
PAL and PPO inhibitors 
Low water temperature 
Low pH 
WATER LOSS(TRANSPIRATION)
A Positive balance will be enhanced by good water uptake and lower transpiration. 
Stomata regulates transpiration 
Its mainly on leaves and stems 
Most fruits and flower petals do nit have stomata 
Leafy veggies does have them 
Stomata is 20- 50 micrometer 
Stomata closes when internal co2 rise, mild water stress.
When there is less water demand , vascular blocages will be less of a problem.
Pretretaments have an effect of stomata too.. (how? was not clear to me) 

Uptake causes 
-Microoganisms 
-Air embolism 
-Wound enzymes 
-Loss causes 
VP diffrence 
-Resistance to water loss 
-Stomata functionality 

No recut !! 
This is possible 
Embolism at cut end is not important as the air will dissolve in the water itself, when the flower is placed in rehydtation solution, leading to a no recut necessity.
Recut is basically to remove bacteria at the stem end and the idea is that biocides in the stem at the farm treatment will kill bacteria at bay! 

Hydrangea stomata 
Hydrangea have alot of leaves hence alot of stomata , that close in the dark but open at light triggering alot of demand for water uptake. So good to keep in the dark in the cold room? 
Good hygiene is very important for this crop 
Embolism accurs more higher up the stem especially as soon as there is water stress. So it happens after a couple of days in the vase.
Warm water period can vary feom seconds to a longer time whith the lower temp. But lower temp may not help in the end , and hot water may damage the stem. 
No much reserch on this but a feeling not really applied .. <50 degrees .. above 50 , you damage the stem.

*ABA (products) causes leaf yellowing but yes , It helps stomata become more functional,... 
Can be used at very low dosages on pot plants , but not applicable to cut flowers due to leaf yellowing problems.

Not all flowers respond well to ice cold water so when applying , please check which crop.

Prolonged expouser of stems to solution (over 4 hrs?) can damage stems? Eg browning? Splitting can be due to osmotic effect that can be treated with sugars.
Cells at the bottom of the stem may take too much water and break due to the osmotic pressure but still this will not cause browning.. so can mover of the question share photos?

23/12 vervolgafspraak

Hi allen,

Zojuist weer telefoongesprek gehad met Patrick (inkoper) en Emiel (kwaliteit). Ze hebben met diverse partijen gesproken waarbij duurzaamheid en kwaliteit belangrijkste was. Dit willen ze in 2026 stap voor stap vormgeven ……….MET ONS!

Krijgen nog uitnodiging om in week 2 of 3 bij elkaar te komen en dit verder te bespreken. Zij komen met voorstellen.

Paar key points:

-	Zij kijken echt kijken naar traject kweker tot en met de winkel. Wat consument doet ligt volgens hun buiten hun bereik. Ik al gezegd dat dit vlgs mij niet zo is en dat we met app en de allerhande hier zeker iets kunnen doen.
-	Vaasleven blijft (voorlopig) bij Flowerwatch. Ze weten dat FW ook indirect concurrent is maar willen wel graag eens in de driehoek Chrysal – AH – FW brainstormen hoe dit beter op te zetten. Ik gezegd dat ik dit intern ga bespreken. Mijn eerste reactie is doen maar niet alles bloot leggen -> hoe staan jullie hierin?
-	Verminderen gebruik gewasbescherming door kwekers. AH gaat rapport van Raymon intern presenteren in week 1. Gaan ook klankboard opzetten met kwekers over wat haalbaar is en wat niet en wil graag dat wij hierin anticiperen en in meedenken. Als we dingen signaleren dit ook proactief laten weten met aanbevelingen
-	Leer module winkelpersoneel in ontwikkeling, gaan dit ook tegen ons aan houden
-	Franchisers nog grijs gebied

subject: A VISIT TO CA/CA TRIALS  BY JANE
description:
Jane visited please find  her comments below! 
The visit was to follow-up of the long storage trials. The crops that had gone through the trials were Eryngium and Aster. To start with, I would like to appreciate the farm teams for the improvements registered in grading standards, which made the work easier for the Chrysal team. The flowers looked great in quality, meeting all the expected grading specifications. Observations entailed the below:
Eryngium Trials.
The varieties included in the trials were White Sirius, Orion, Magnetar, Aquarius, and Scopious. We also had fresh flowers from the farm for comparison. The flowers looked similar in quality and blooming; however, the leaf quality of the C/A flowers appeared greener and fresher.
On Magnetar, the leaves from the C/A samples showed signs of dehydration from the bottom upwards, while the fresh bunch from the farm maintained green and healthy leaves.
Scopious looked good both from C/A and fresh samples from the farm. However, the C/A Scopious flowers appeared to develop a green extension at the end of every cone, as if fresh/new growth was developing.
In conclusion, I think in Eryngium we have made a big and positive step. Note: going forward, we will re-look at the Orion cut stage since both the C/A and fresh flowers appeared slightly advanced in cut stage.
Aster Crop:
The varieties in the trial were DD/P, Pink Flush, and Dark Milk. In these trials, some work is still pending before achieving the three expected parameters, i.e., leaf quality, blooming, and colour. However, across the trials, about 75% of the C/A flowers registered improvements in leaf quality and blooming. The change of colour after blooming remains a challenge (Bram to offer more guidance on this).
I believe we are keen on fine details going forward, especially on the cold chain, and the improvements will be well reflected in the next trials once the flowers are removed from the cold chambers.
Otherwise, I note very good collaboration between Chrysal and the farms, timely communication, and I appreciate the assignment of following up assigned to me by my leader which I find very beneficial.

We  adjusted farm training dates to next year 
We will do Gyp trials with GVB at Ku denga farm

subject: Nog iets gehoord op voorstel AA / Blume 2000 gestuurd op 12/12
description:
Hello Marcel, Henrik and Geurt,

Henrik and Geurt, thanks again for your warm welcome and to give me the opportunity to talk about our products and services.

We know that our products and services are the best. Blume 2000 did a recently a comparison between Chrysal flower food, Vaselife and another competitor and came to conclusion that Chrysal flower food performed best and that they will keep on ordering the Blume 2000 sachets via Chrysal. Also we of course comply to all regulations regarding ingredients, packaging etc.

I understand that next to quality and service, price is also an important factor. I value our cooperation as well and would like to continue working together. I have investigated the prices and current conditions and below you see our proposal. 

-	We combine buying power from Blume 2000 / Bloominess Polska / Alex Andersen
-	2 year agreement
-	Fixed prices for 2026, see below, and for 2027, if price changes need to change, we use CPI as possible maximum change
-	Payment terms stay 30 days 

Professional 2 prices

You now use the 200L and 600L barrels.. 
458965868 Chrysal Prof. 2 Concentrated barrel 1*200L 5ml/L INT euro
458920308 Chrysal Prof. 2 Conc. barrel 1*600L 5ml/L EN/PL/DE euro
458465868 Chrysal Prof. 2 Concentrated barrel 1*200L 5ml/L INT

With the above mentioned conditions, I already could lower the price but if ordered in bigger quantities at once I and all the barrels to be picked up in Naarden I can do a bit more, see below.

458965868 Chrysal Prof. 2 Concentrated barrel 1*200L 5ml/L INT euro

458465868 Chrysal Prof. 2 Concentrated barrel 1*200L 5ml/L INT
	1-4 barrels	€ 627,50
	5-8 barrels	€ 596,13
	9-12 barrels	€ 580,44
		
458920308 Chrysal Prof. 2 Conc. barrel 1*600L 5ml/L EN/PL/DE euro
	1-4 barrels	€ 1645,00
	5-8 barrels	€ 1562,75
	9-12 barrels	€ 1521,63


As told, we are now testing a new professional 2 where the storage/transport phase can be extended with 2 days. The tests look promising. If we introduce this new variant, we will introduce it next to our current proven formula.  I will of course keep you posted on this.


343051553 Blume 2000 Bio Based Univ. paper sachet 1500*1L DE

Recently I lowered the price already to € 54,27 per box. Also have looked into this price again and with above conditions I can also lower this price to € 53,38.

Another opportunity here is transport. We monthly sent 360 boxes from Naarden to Hamburg. This is 12 pallets and to be open and transparent we pay € 604,00 for this. If these 12 pallets also can be picked up by Alex Andersen and they arrange transport, we can give this as a discount back. This is another € 1,68 per box. I understand that also Alex Andersen needs to get paid for transport to Hamburg but with a combined transport this could be cheaper. Condition is that minimum order is 12 pallets. 

Services
We will stay in regular contact about our cooperation and your forecast for coming period so we keep on making sure all deliveries will be in time so a smooth operations is guaranteed. Also we can help you to protect your quality by doing audits and or give trainings. This year we already have given a training to personnel of Alex Andersen.

During our cooperation we can offer once per year:
-	An audit or training  at Bloominess Polska
-	An audit or training at Alex Andersen
-	An audit at 5 Flower shops of Blume 2000

I expect to have give you a good offer here and am of course willing to talk about it in a face 2 face talk.

Looking forward to your reaction

subject: Visit
description:
Farm visit to see the progress on the Gypsophila in the new warm room. 
There was no or very little browning on the flowers which was a very huge step for the farm.
Doreen had advised we try out GVB with Borehole water and the soft water GVB too. 
Previously, the GVB gave a pH of 2.5 with dam water. 
Tried the soft water GVB with dam water and the pH was 3.49. 
GVB with borehole water gave a pH of 3.54 (perfect). Soft water GVB with borehole water gave a pH of 5.49. 
Agreed with Doreen and Karuga that they can use the GVB already at the farm with borehole water.
Flowers that were put in the GVB+dam water didn't have any browning at the base despite the low pH.

subject: Visit
description:
Farm visit with Margriet, Frans and Matt.
Met with Amnoni and Peter. Discussed the STS and Matt will follow up with a competitive offer. 
The farm is looking into trialling the Limoniums for long storage using the Chrysal chambers. 
Agreed to do one more trial for Unicorn and send to Pini NL. Margriet will visit Pini to have a look at the flowers. 
Another trial on Asclepias with Prof 2, Prof 3 and RVBc+Optigyp was recommended as the farm is still struggling with the orange variety. Yellow variety performance is better in the market. Currently, they give Asclepias flower food once they arrive in the market. 
For Leucanthemums, the farm is looking for uptake dyes.  They asked about Sunflower dip dye especially the red colour. The farm was to share the exact red colour code for the dip dye. 
Agreed also to try out RVBc on Limonium to see if RVBc can compete with TOG 3 in performance. 
There was an observation made during the meeting that Limonium grown in the open fields smell less compared to the ones grown in greenhouses.

subject: Visit
description:
Farm visit to check the RoseDip progress. The farm had stopped dipping in RoseDip as they were not allowed to mix RoseDip with insecticides. They mixed Dipnoy with the insecticide to control the thrips. 
Margriet gave a go ahead to mix but exonerated Chrysal from any flowers quality issues that might follow later.  
The farm mixed RoseDip with Radiant @ 0.5mls/L, RoseDip with Matrix 0.5mls/L and RoseDip with Acetak @ 0.3gms/L. Only one mix showed phyto on leaves, but the insecticide rate was reduced and no phyto was seen. 
The farm will keep trialing with the mixes so as to have an informed decision if they decide to go fully commercial with the RoseDip+insecticide.

subject: Visit
description:
Farm visit to carry out more trials on Asclepias, Unicorn and Limonium.
Asclepias:
The treatments were as follows:
T1-----Prof 2 @ 5mls/L+BB5,
T2-----Prof 3 @ 10mls/L+BB5 and 
T3-----RVBc @ 1ml/L+Sugar @ 50mls/L+BB5.
Note that the flowers were harvested in hot water, graded and back in hot water for 30 minutes, then in TOG 75 pulsing@ 8mls/L then into the respective treatments. 

Unicorn:
T1---farm treatment TOG L101
T2---RVBc @ 1ml/L+BVB @ 0.15mls/L.
For Unicorn, the flowers were to be shipped to NL attention Pini. Margriet was to visit and have a look at the flowers upon confirmation. 

Limonium:
T1----Farm treatment TOG3+TOG75+Sugar+Tivag and 
T2---RVBc @ 1ml/L+TOG75+Sugar+Tivag.
The aim is to compare TOG 3 and RVBc performances and see if RVBc can replace TOG 3 as it is competitive price wise. 
The Asclepias and Limonium were left at the farm for observation. 
The farm will give feedback on a spray done on Limonium to neutralise their smell. The sample was given by Margriet.

subject: KARIKI BONDET - Farm Audit
description:
We audited the farm 
In summary- Hygiene results of this audit had many gaps, therefore  a need for closer look and action. 
Most Post harvest solutions were polluted. Make sure mixing containers are cleaned and disinfected before a new solution is made/mixed.
For Hypericum , adding a disinfecting product in the water is advised. Whereas the crop may not be very sensitive to bacteria pollution, it pollutes vase water, affecting other sensitive flowers in a bouquet.

Harvest secateurs, cleaned buckets,  grading hall tables and guillotines were polluted. Harvesters and graders are advised to follow the laid out disinfecting procedures provided, for best result.
Please also re-check the gaps in bucket washing process 
Check and do timely servicing of the cold room coolers.

subject: VISIT
description:
ACTION: DFG DIPPING AND FOLLOW UPS
ABL/BVL sites
•	These two sites have a clear dipping system and product flow is quite clear.
•	Finished product dipping for some lines is well laid out while raw material dipping for the other lines is also well organised.
•	Since the last visit with Patrick, they seem to be following on the agreed protocol.
•	QC’s stationed at intake are quite familiar with their work and are doing follow ups.
•	The dipping team is quite good and the dipping in general good.
•	Hope they do what they were doing when DFG/Chrysal were present because it looked quite good and they were not aware of the visit.
•	The dipping area is also quite spacious so there should be no room for mix up.

subject: VISIT
description:
ATION: VISIT DFG FOLLOW UP
•	There was a fair trade audit during this visit so they were not showing or publicly dipping.
•	Even then, product flow not good and chances of mix up are very high[ Dipped and non-dipped]
•	Flowers are still dried outside after dipping not with the blowers but naturally. We instructed to dip and straight into the coldroom.
•	Dipped flowers are kept in a separate shelf at the intake cold room but the room is small so what happens when the production is high?
•	The farm believes they do everything right and they do not understand where the problem is coming from.
•	From the observation; there could be a problem at the end of the chain where undipped flowers are used to fulfil the orders or there is a mix up during processing.
•	The suggestion was to have a full time QC to verify and  supervise DFG dipping and the person becomes fully answerable. He or she also supervises processing the order the following day before again going to check on the days dipping.
